original_abstract:
This study investigated the influence of microwave power levels during vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) on the drying kinetics, temperature profiles, and final quality of sour cherries. Quality was assessed based on phenolic compound retention, antioxidant capacity, and color stability. For comparison, cherries were also processed via convective drying (CD) at 50, 60, and 70 °C, while freeze drying (FD) served as the control. To prevent overheating at the critical moisture content of approximately 1 kg/kg dry mass, VMD was conducted by reducing initial power settings (240, 360, and 480 W) to lower levels (240 and 120 W).

The results indicated that higher air temperatures during CD and elevated material temperatures during VMD generally led to the degradation of phenolic compounds, a drop in antioxidant activity, and less desirable color changes—effects closely linked to anthocyanin loss. However, VMD proved significantly more efficient than CD and remained competitive with FD in terms of product quality. The optimal results, characterized by the highest nutrient retention and superior color, were achieved using VMD at an initial 480 W reduced to 120 W. Notably, the drying process actually enhanced the detectable levels of quercetin and kaempferol derivatives. Ultimately, VMD offers a high-quality, shelf-stable alternative to fresh sour cherries that maintains much of the fruit's functional value.
humanized_abstract:
The effects of microwave power on the drying behaviour, temperature gradient, and retention of constituents (such as phenolic compounds) and their subsequent impact on antioxidant activity and color stability in sour cherries dried by vacuum-microwave drying (VMD) were investigated. In addition, convective drying (CD) at 50, 60 and 70 °C and freeze drying (FD) were also applied on sour cherries as references. At a moisture content of about 1 kg/kg dry matter, VMD samples were exposed to thermal overshooting and their initial microwave powers (240, 360 and 480 W) were reduced (to 240 and 120 W).

After drying, the cherry samples were evaluated for changes in content of phenolic compounds, antioxidant activity, colour and appearance. All of these attributes deteriorated with increasing air temperature during CD and with increasing material temperature during VMD due to a large reduction in anthocyanin content. VMD was however superior to CD and nearly as good as FD in terms of product quality. The best product quality after drying was obtained when the initial power of 480 W in VMD was reduced to 120 W after 30 min of drying. All drying processes increased the levels of quercetin and kaempferol derivatives. Thus, VMD process affords a high quality, shelf-stable product in terms of quality and function compared to the fresh product.
